rows.stage_1_card_label: Questions, stories and bigger chats
rows.product_or_action_name: Pippa Goodhart & Nick Sharratt — You Choose
rows.ui_description: A picture book packed with choices on every page, inviting your child to pick, point and explain as you read together at bedtime or on the sofa.
rows.why_pip_picked_this: Page after page of choices for your child to pick and explain. A lovely way to keep storytime interactive as their ideas grow. Lots of nurseries already know this one.
rows.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread , a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question , so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.
rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order in this category. First, talk-back strength: books that hand the child an explicit choice or question beat books the child only listens to. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a publisher or retailer age mark that genuinely spans 34-36 months, not a bare interest age that starts at 5. Third, ownership risk: You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books are UK household staples, so they rank as borrow-first even when they win on educational fit. Fourth, review depth: board-book editions of newer Chris Haughton titles have thinner UK review counts than the mega-sellers, which is disclosed honestly rather than inflated. Fifth, format and freshness: board editions (Pigeon, Oh No George, Shh We Have a Plan) survive nearly-three handling better than paperback picture books, and Shark in the Park was kept in Top 5 as the lowest-effort entry point when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime.

how_trail.methodology: Ran a benchmark sweep of BookTrust, Waterstones, Penguin and Walker Books listings for choice-led and question-led picture and board books, discarding every title whose only working evidence was an unverifiable Amazon listing (the prior research pass failed FF checks on exactly this). Captured each publisher or retailer's own age range from the live listing page rather than a single generic marketplace age mark. Ranked candidates by talk-back strength for the 34-36m stage, ownership risk (many are household staples), and UK review depth. Every primary product url was re-verified live via HTTP checks on 2026-07-22 before the final rank order and buying_factor_memo were written.

rows.stage_1_card_label: Feelings, friends and turn-taking
rows.product_or_action_name: Camilla Reid & Axel Scheffler — Pip and Posy: The New Friend
rows.ui_description: A warm Pip and Posy picture book where Posy finds Pip playing with someone new, then finds her feet again in a story about friendship and joining in.
rows.why_pip_picked_this: Helps your child learn about situations where their best friend scuttles away to play with someone else. Posy finds her feet again, and the story stays warm and light the whole way through.
rows.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: third-friend jealousy is the sharpest 34, 36m social wobble and Posy’s self-repair beats Little Whale’s sharing lesson on specificity, even though both clear the friendship brief.
rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order. First, social-repair fit: stories where two or three children negotiate sharing, jealousy or sorry beat solo feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a listing age mark spanning 34, 36 months ,  bare “from 5 years” interest ages were rejected. Third, scene specificity: Pip & Posy’s third-friend jealousy and Little Whale’s snack-sharing row map closer to real playdates than abstract kindness metaphors. Fourth, review depth: newer titles (Little Whale) stay in only where editorial comparables and 15+ UK reviews clear the bar. Fifth, buyability: primaries must be purchasable today ,  On Sudden Hill moved to longlist backup after availability check flagged out of stock on the only stable UK primary.

rows.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, age-gate clearance: band min is 34 months, so any listing with not suitable under 3 years or a bare 3+ only mark failed regardless of reviews ,  that eliminated Melissa & Doug, Bigjigs, Jaques and most Galt sets despite their popularity. Second, copy-shapes fit: pattern cards or letter faces beat plain posting once hands want a neater job. Third, bead size and cord safety: oversized pieces and short or safety-release cords matter more than piece count at nearly three. Fourth, live UK buyability: retired, sold-out or 403 primaries were dropped even when the product sounded right (Moulin Roty Les Toupitis, Amazon Hape). Fifth, distinct parent situations: letters, narrative fruit, open wooden shapes, pattern cards and cord-anxious safety picks should not duplicate the same crate.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order in this category. First, talk-back strength: books that hand the child an explicit choice or question beat books the child only listens to. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a publisher or retailer age mark that genuinely spans 34-36 months, not a bare interest age that starts at 5. Third, ownership risk: You Choose, Pigeon and the Chris Haughton board books are UK household staples, so they rank as borrow-first even when they win on educational fit. Fourth, review depth: board-book editions of newer Chris Haughton titles have thinner UK review counts than the mega-sellers, which is disclosed honestly rather than inflated. Fifth, format and freshness: board editions (Pigeon, Oh No George, Shh We Have a Plan) survive nearly-three handling better than paperback picture books, and Shark in the Park was kept in Top 5 as the lowest-effort entry point when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime.
category_how.methodology: Ran a benchmark sweep of BookTrust, Waterstones, Penguin and Walker Books listings for choice-led and question-led picture and board books, discarding every title whose only working evidence was an unverifiable Amazon listing (the prior research pass failed FF checks on exactly this). Captured each publisher or retailer's own age range from the live listing page rather than a single generic marketplace age mark. Ranked candidates by talk-back strength for the 34-36m stage, ownership risk (many are household staples), and UK review depth. Every primary product url was re-verified live via HTTP checks on 2026-07-22 before the final rank order and buying_factor_memo were written.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: You Choose asks the child to pick and explain on every spread , a more open-ended prompt than Pigeon's single repeated question , so it edges ahead on educational fit despite carrying higher ownership risk.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus! (board book)
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #2 over #3: Pigeon's comic call-and-response (say no, explain why) is a stronger nightly talk-back trigger than Oh No George's gentler what-next question, and it has deeper UK review history.
#3 over #4: George's repeated what-will-he-do-next beat keeps talk-back going page after page, edging out Shh's quieter suspense which leans more on prediction than explanation.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: Shh! We Have a Plan (board book)
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #4 over #5: Shh's silent-plan suspense still asks the child to predict and explain, which sits closer to the cluster's talk-back objective than Shark in the Park's simpler spot-the-shark refrain.

#5: Included as the lightest, most repeatable option , a good entry point for what-do-you-see chat when the other four feel too talky for a tired bedtime, even though it asks less explanation than ranks 1-4.
#6: sits just below Shark in the Park — a similar prediction format but a slightly gentler pace for daily talk-back.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Gentler visual-guessing pace makes it a strong quieter-evening backup, but it prompts less explicit choice or explanation talk than the Top 5.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #7: ranks above Would You Rather because its branching plot lets the child invent more freely, but its thin review count keeps it out of Top 5 for now.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Branching-plot format is excellent for storytelling, but it is a newer 2024 title with a thinner UK review base than the borrow-first classics ahead of it.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #8: ahead of You Choose Bedtime because its dilemmas are more explanation-led than a bedtime-only spin-off of the book already ranked #1.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Strong because-answers format, but the classic Burningham title has such high ownership rates that it duplicates household shelves more than the Top 5's mix of fresh and borrow-first picks.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #9: ranks above You Choose Your Dreams as the more targeted bedtime routine tie-in, though both are variations on the #1 pick's format.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Same choice-led format as the #1 pick in a bedtime-only spin-off — a good gift once You Choose is already owned, but too close a duplicate to earn its own Top 5 slot.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #10: ranks just above Oi Frog! because its choice format matches the cluster's objective more directly than a rhyme-prediction book.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: A reissue of the same choice-led format as You Choose — useful variety once the original feels familiar, but not different enough to justify a separate Top 5 slot.

category_how.stage_1_card_label: Pretend play with people, places and feelings
category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order. First, whole-scene fit: sets that make get-in, go, help and return easy beat lone vehicles or build-only packs. Second, age overlap: only Happyland / DUPLO 2+ / Playmobil Junior 1-4 ,  never classic Playmobil under-3 exclusions. Third, figure quality: chunky people the child can seat and move. Fourth, ownership risk: Happyland buses and common DUPLO vehicles are cupboard staples, so borrow-first language stays honest even when they win on fit. Fifth, review honesty: where UK review widgets would not scrape cleanly (Happyland on Moonpig; Playmobil Junior brand pages; retired DUPLO Bus Ride screened out by availability gate), specialist exemption is declared with a written reason instead of inventing counts; DUPLO Construction Vehicles keeps a hard Amazon UK rating (4.8 / 287).
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK toddler small-world lines (Happyland, DUPLO Town 2+, Playmobil Junior 1-4) against the whole-scene brief of get-in / go / help / return, discarding classic Playmobil and any set whose live age mark failed the 34-36m band. Captured age signals from brand or retailer pages (Moonpig 12 months+, LEGO/Hamleys 2+, Playmobil Junior 1-4). Ranked by scene strength for people-places-feelings pretend, ownership risk, and review depth where scrapeable. Every Top 5 primary URL was re-verified live via stage3-url-preflight.mjs (HTTP 200) on 2026-07-22 before the final order and buying_factor_memo were locked. Availability screening (stage3-availability-check.mjs) added 2026-07-22 to reject retired/discontinued and notify-when-in-stock primaries.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: Happyland hands the child ready-made people and a stop for get-on / go / return with zero build friction, so it edges DUPLO Bus Ride when the brief is whole-scene pretend rather than brick play.
#2 over #3: Airport board-and-go keeps the travel story from #1 but adds a different setting; it replaces retired/out-of-stock DUPLO Bus Ride 10988 after availability screening failed that SKU.
#3 over #4: Help-and-rescue roles match the cluster feelings brief more tightly than dump-truck load/tip play, so Everyday Heroes sits above the Dump Truck despite similar Junior age marks.
#4 over #5: One figure plus a tipping bed gives a clearer single job story than the multi-rebuild Construction Vehicles set, even though 10475 has stronger Amazon UK ratings.
#5 as the ratings-backed rebuild option: it earns the last Top 5 slot on review depth and 2+ age fit, while sitting behind clearer people-scene sets on educational match.
Strong board / return animal scene on Junior age marks.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Excellent get-on / return play, but animal-ark storytelling sits slightly beside the people-places-feelings helper brief that Everyday Heroes covers more directly, and the price is higher.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: Airport board/go scene on Junior marks.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors set order for nearly-three first jigsaws. First, real interlocking strategy: 6, 12 piece scenes with a visible picture beat peg boards and 2-piece warm-ups already mastered. Second, age overlap with 34, 36m: Orchard/Ravensburger/Bigjigs lines marked 2+ or 18m+ pass FF age gates; honest 3+ only marks (Big Wheels, Galt 4-in-a-box) stay on the longlist. Third, progressive value: a box that steps 4→12 or mixes 2/3-piece bridges before 12-piece wins shelf space. Fourth, review depth on working UK primaries ,  Amazon and Orchard on-site where scrapeable. Fifth, distinct parent jobs: farm 12-piece plan, jungle theme swap, thick-card warm-up, wooden durability, and emergency-vehicle quick wins without five near-duplicate farm sets.
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked UK toddler jigsaw lines (Orchard First Friends, Ravensburger My First, Bigjigs 6-piece wood, Galt 4-in-a-box) against the brief: peg puzzles out, 2, 12 real interlocking pieces with picture-first completion. Captured structured age_signals from live brand/Amazon pages (2+, 18m+, manufacturer 24m). Ranked with buying_factor_memo for strategy fit, age honesty, evidence, and gift variety. Every Top 5 primary was smoke-checked with stage3-url-preflight.mjs and stage3-availability-check.mjs (all buyable HTTP 200) on 2026-07-22 before locking order.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: Farm Friends is the clearest 12-piece strategy step-up with the deepest Amazon proof; jungle theme at #2 is the same spec so farm wins as the default first buy.
#2 over #3: Jungle Friends matches the core 12-piece strategy job; it edges Ravensburger My First because the box mark is honestly 2+ rather than an 18m warm-up line.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: On The Farm — My First Jigsaw Puzzles
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #3 over #4: Ravensburger thick-card warm-ups build turn-compare-try with massive review proof before the wooden 6-piece trays; wood wins durability but fewer aggregated UK reviews.
#4 over #5: Wooden 6-piece interlock is a distinct material/format with honest 2+ mark; Rescue Squad is brilliant for quick wins but only 2, 3 pieces , less strategy for confident nearly-threes.

#5 completes the set: Rescue Squad covers cautious starters and vehicle fans when 12-piece feels a stretch; it trails wood and Ravensburger warm-ups on strategy depth but wins on hook and Amazon proof.
Rank 6: same 12-piece Orchard format as Top 5 themes — strong but redundant if farm or jungle already chosen.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Near-duplicate of ranks 1–2; only buy if dinosaurs are the obsession and neither farm nor jungle is owned.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: Rank 7: excellent progressive vehicle set but 3+ age mark fails strictest 34–36m FF overlap.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Age 3+ manufacturer mark excludes the youngest months in this band; Rescue Squad covers vehicles at 2+ instead.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: Rank 8: strong value progressive box but 3+ age gate and thinner card vs Orchard/Ravensburger.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Honest 3+ and under-36-months choking label overlap the youngest nearly-threes; Galt direct URL also 404 for agents — Amazon primary used.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: Rank 9: Ravensburger quality 2x12 farm — overlaps rank 1 with a stricter 3+ mark.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Duplicates Orchard Farm Friends at the same 12-piece job with a less band-friendly 3+ age mark.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: Rank 10: lovely character ladder but 3+ and up to 24 pieces stretches past first-jigsaw brief.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: 24-piece top rung is a stretch for this Stage 2 card; 3+ mark and higher price vs core Orchard First Friends.

category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order. First, social-repair fit: stories where two or three children negotiate sharing, jealousy or sorry beat solo feelings charts. Second, age overlap: every Top Pick needed a listing age mark spanning 34, 36 months ,  bare “from 5 years” interest ages were rejected. Third, scene specificity: Pip & Posy’s third-friend jealousy and Little Whale’s snack-sharing row map closer to real playdates than abstract kindness metaphors. Fourth, review depth: newer titles (Little Whale) stay in only where editorial comparables and 15+ UK reviews clear the bar. Fifth, buyability: primaries must be purchasable today ,  On Sudden Hill moved to longlist backup after availability check flagged out of stock on the only stable UK primary.
category_how.methodology: Benchmarked Mother & Baby sharing roundups, Nosy Crow, Hachette, Little Tiger and Scholastic Shop listings for friendship-conflict and turn-taking plots (not emotion labelling). Captured structured age_signals from each live primary listing. Ranked with buying_factor_memo for social-repair fit, age overlap at 34, 36m, UK review depth and ownership risk. Every Top 5 primary was bench-ranked then url-verified live (HTTP 200 smoke + stage3-availability-check.mjs buyable) on 2026-07-22 before locking order ,  On Sudden Hill dropped from Top 5 after Scholastic showed out of stock.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #1 over #2: third-friend jealousy is the sharpest 34, 36m social wobble and Posy’s self-repair beats Little Whale’s sharing lesson on specificity, even though both clear the friendship brief.
#2 over #3: Little Whale’s snack-and-attention sharing row is fresher and more playdate-specific than the chair-territory fight in That’s (Not) Mine, which many families already know.
#3 over #4: the mine-shouting chair fight gives parents a same-day script after toy grabs, edging ahead of Sour Grape’s forgiveness arc which lands better once the row has cooled.
#4 over #5: forgiveness after a friend fallout is a harder nearly-three skill than competitive sharing, so Sour Grape ranks above Squirrels even though both model repair.
#5: competitive nut-chase teamwork is a strong backup social-repair story but less playdate-specific than the jealousy, sharing and mine-fight picks above.
#6: strongest three-way jealousy story in the bench but unbuyable on the stable UK primary at check time.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Dropped from Top 5: Scholastic primary showed out of stock on 2026-07-22 availability check — keep as backup when stock returns.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #7 over #8: mutual sorry after a friend accident maps closer to playdate bust-ups than scooter snatching alone.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Apology arc is brilliant but slightly less peer-play-specific than Top 5 friendship rows.

ranks_1_to_10.product_name: Pip and Posy: The Super Scooter
ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #8: same-world Pip & Posy repair as #1 but narrower snatch plot and higher ownership overlap.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Excellent snatch-and-sorry but families with Pip & Posy often already own this one.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #9: playground loneliness backup when the issue is joining in, not snatching.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: Gentle inclusion story but lighter on turn-taking conflict than Top 5.

ranks_1_to_10.rank_rationale: #10: better when the wobble is making a new friend, not sharing with one you already have.
ranks_1_to_10.missed_top5_reason: First-friendship nerves rather than mid-play conflict repair.

category_how.stage_1_card_label: Threading, snipping, drawing and dressing hands
category_how.buying_factor_memo: Five factors decided order here. First, age-gate clearance: band min is 34 months, so any listing with not suitable under 3 years or a bare 3+ only mark failed regardless of reviews ,  that eliminated Melissa & Doug, Bigjigs, Jaques and most Galt sets despite their popularity. Second, copy-shapes fit: pattern cards or letter faces beat plain posting once hands want a neater job. Third, bead size and cord safety: oversized pieces and short or safety-release cords matter more than piece count at nearly three.
